summary: Martin Fowler analyzes traffic patterns to martinfowler.com from 2011-2023, revealing that traffic peaked around 2020 and has since declined to 2015 levels. The analysis shows extremely skewed distribution where 10% of pages generate over 80% of traffic, with traffic dropping exponentially after publication but stabilizing after six months. Notably, older articles continue to perform well, with posts over five years old generating about half of all post traffic - validating Fowler's strategy of prioritizing lasting content over churn. Weekend traffic is significantly lower than weekday traffic, and December shows consistent drops due to holidays.
key_points: Traffic peaked around 2020 with steady growth from 2011, followed by equally steady decline to 2015 levels by 2023
Article traffic follows an extreme exponential decay after publication - so steep it's exponential even on a logarithmic scale - but stabilizes after about six months
The top 10% of pages generate over 80% of site traffic, demonstrating highly skewed distribution
Older content remains valuable: posts over 5 years old still generate about half of all post traffic, validating the evergreen content strategy
Traffic is predominantly weekday-driven, with clear weekend drops and consistent December holiday dips
money_quotes: Traffic levels in 2023 were about the same as 2015.
Not just is it an exponential curve, it's an exponential curve when plotted on logarithmic scale.
The top 10% of pages generated just over 80% of the traffic on the site.
I've always preferred to concentrate on material with longer lasting value.
Posts that are over five years old get about half the traffic that comes to posts on the site.
Although that's the main thing to note here I should not forget that younger posts do grab a big hunk, 13% of traffic came from brand new articles.
key_insight: Investing in evergreen content that provides lasting value pays off significantly - older articles continue generating substantial traffic years after publication, contrary to the conventional wisdom that drives content churn.
